author: leolara
content:
The STEEL blog post on the testing method some of us worked on a while back is finally out: Specs as Models: Applying Category-Partition Testing to Ethereum's Consensus Layer. The writing and review took a while, so it is landing well after the work itself.

https://steel.ethereum.foundation/blog/2026-06-11_specs-as-models/

The idea in one line: the consensus specs are executable Python, so we read them as a formal model and enumerated the input scenarios of each function systematically. Every `assert` is a partition boundary. Every `if` a dimension of variation. From there we got a precise, auditable answer to a question we usually answer by intuition, "how complete are the tests for this function?"

author: leolara
content:
What it produced when we applied it:

process_proposer_slashing: 25 distinct scenarios, 18 covered by the suite at the time (72%). The gaps became 20 new tests in PR [#4871](https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/pull/4871), coverage up to 96%.
While documenting process_withdrawals for Gloas, the analysis surfaced a real spec defect: update_next_withdrawal_validator_index did not handle the BUILDER_INDEX_FLAG. It showed up during documentation, before any fuzzing or manual review (PR #4835).
The method held across three different function shapes: sequential validation, a multi-phase pipeline, and routing logic. The post also covers loop handling.
One part I found most useful: the partition tables can be handed to AI agents as a precise spec for test generation. That is what makes it scale across many functions during a fork.

A bit of history that I like. This method is old, and it used to be more automatic than what we did here. Dick and Faivre showed in 1993 how to take a formal spec in VDM, reduce its predicates to disjunctive normal form, and let a tool derive the partitions on its own. The catch was that the formal spec had to live separately from the code, the same cost that made eth2.0-dafny such a large effort. Python specs are executable, so they avoid that second model, and the partitioning falls back to people and AI doing it by hand. A formal and executable language like Lean 4 could in principle bring that automation back on a single source of truth. I have been exploring this on my own with an foundation in Lean 4 (Etheorem https://github.com/etheorem/etheorem), as a personal research interest.

The post is written so the method can be picked up and applied by anyone who wants it. The full procedure is there: the spec-doc step, the partition tables, and the worked examples.

Thanks to <@1019999229151821936> and <@547171287831805972> for the review and help.
